item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this, but the writing is too wordy and redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whilst I like this SCP, there are a few things wrong with it.

Firstly, the Foundation does not seem to be interested in finding out whether or not this alters perception or reveals reality; that's an important point to figure out, because figuring that out informs the entire course of action. If it's the first, you just lock the bathroom and make sure no-one enters or exits it. If it's the second, someone has systematically kidnapped, surgically altered and brainwashed up to 2000 people (I'm assuming this is the Dallas Zoo, based on the source of the picture), before placing a memetic… something on the place, meaning that anyone who entered it only saw his victims as animals. That's someone the Foundation needs to find.

There are many ways of resolving this ambiguity; firstly, examine organ and skeletal structures. Say, are a "humanified" bird's bones still pneumatised or does a "humanified" lizard still have a polikothermic organ system? Secondly, take the DNA of an instance and check to see if it's the animal it's supposed to be (if a "humanified" zebra is, skeletally and genetically, a zebra, it's probably a zebra).

Also, as stated above, the first paragraph needs some rejigging. But, I do like this SCP as a concept; it's like a reverse Chicken Boo from Animaniacs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That possibility was plenty disturbing, but considering that the description implies that they had Foundation agents go through SCP-2429-1, and inspect and try to interview the animals, and despite being freaked out the Foundation eventually concluded that it was just an illusion (and they have plenty experience with this kind of thing - see SCP-2480 for instance) - I'm very inclined to trust their judgement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the concept and it got my vote. However, I would really like to see more. For example, if they allow a D class to experience the effects, can he physically interact with the humans/animals? Does his hand pass through or does it contact the strange bone profusions that do not seem to exist to us? Deciding on either one clears up uncertainty (personally the second choice is much more unsettling to me, as instead of the bathroom tricking viewers it is actually revealing the truth).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't decide if this is more hilarious or horrifying. It's like a reverse Chicken Boo from animaniacs.

"That's not a chicken, It's a tiny man!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing could use some work. In general there's a lot of "this happened, then so-and-so said this, then…", which is dull. You're telling us things that happened in the most boring way possible. Show, don't tell.

An overly detailed analysis of the first paragraph of the description.
– hide block

SCP-2429 is The ████████ Zoo located in ████████, Texas.

This is absolutely fine.

The zoo is, for all intents and purposes, a normal zoo.

This is the worst sentence in the whole article. The phrase "for all intents and purposes" is one of those space-filling expressions that really don't mean anything. Not only do you not need it, it dilutes the article with boring. But if you delete it, all that's left is "The zoo is a normal zoo", which is both terrible writing and is entirely wrong. If it was a normal zoo it wouldn't be an SCP! I know it's not a normal zoo, as does every other reader, why are you saying that it is?

All staff and guests interact with the zoo as would be expected and no anomalous properties are immediately noticeable.

"The zoo is a normal zoo." You said that already.

The anomalous properties of SCP-2429 manifest only after entering and exiting a bathroom on the far east side of the zoo, hereafter referred to as SCP-2429-1.

"Entering and exiting" is mildly redundant, since you obviously can't exit without first having entered, but this isn't awful. I would have made the bathroom be the main item and the animals the -1s, but what you did is fine.

Upon entering and exiting SCP-2429-1 all of the animals contained within SCP-2429 appear as nude humans.

There's the same enter/exit issue, but the real problem is "appear as". The anomalous effect doesn't change the animals generally, it changes how they appear to the specific person who exited the bathroom. So say that: "Upon exiting SCP-2429-1, affected individuals will perceive the animals as…"

This appearance manifests only for those who have entered and exited SCP-2429-1 and has no effect on other observers.

You only need this sentence because the previous one didn't work. If you fix that one, this becomes redundant.

The humans continue to exhibit behaviors typical of whatever animal would normally be in their enclosure, including crawling on all fours, eating raw meat or plant matter, animalistic vocalizations, and so on.

Again, it's the viewer that's changed, not the animal/humans (whichever). It doesn't make sense for them to "continue" their behavior.

Speaking of which, it's a great idea to leave the reader unsure whether they're animals or humans, but the article needs to be consistent. You can't call them "animals" in one sentence and "humans" in the next. The in-universe Foundation researcher that's writing the page knows which it is, or thinks he does; the article needs to reflect that. Plus, you don't want to make it obvious that there's a mystery; you want to hint it at. Show, don't tell.

The effects of SCP-2429-1 seem to be localized to SCP-2429 and do not effect animals elsewhere. Birds, rodents, or other animals that enter SCP-2429 still appear as they normally would even to those who have passed through SCP-2429-1.

Once more: the animals don't change, the people do. Now that this horse is thoroughly dead, I should point out that my suggestion of designating the animals as -1 would make this part unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made some small changes to clarify some confusion brought up in the discussion here. Hopefully the idea is more clear now. I also plan to add experiment logs to answer even more questions but I'll run those through the drafts forums first.

Thanks again to all the positive feedback and criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I see what everyone is saying. I've been mulling it over and I plan to go back and make it all more clear soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really convinced there's any ambiguity here. This is supposedly based around the perception of the -1s, but the rest of the article is really unclear as to who's providing what details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a couple of instances where the wording/sentence structure is a bit awkward:

+ Nitpickies
- Oh noes hides them

The humans continue to exhibit behaviors typical of whatever animal would normally be in their enclosure. Including behaviors such as crawling on all fours, eating raw meat or plant matter, animalistic vocalizations, and so on.

could be:

The humans continue to exhibit behaviors typical of whatever animal would normally be in their enclosure, including crawling on all fours, eating raw meat or plant matter, animalistic vocalizations, and so on.

and:

Claiming that the previous owner had constructed a wooden barrier in front of all entrances to SCP-2429-1 and strongly advised ignoring SCP-2429-1 rather than "attempting to repair it."

could be:

[He/She/They/The current owner] claimed that the previous owner had constructed a wooden barrier in front of all entrances to SCP-2429-1 and strongly advised ignoring SCP-2429-1 rather than "attempting to repair it."

I also agree with Accelerando that the "closer inspection" bit, being vague on whether those doing the inspecting are affected, seems to push it pretty hard in the "they really are humans" direction.

EDIT: Put stuff in collapsible because that was more page-eaty than expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting skip, I'll give you that. The writing is a tad rough, though, and I agree with above, that it seems the anomaly is the bathroom and not the entire zoo. Do people who experience this have the same effect happen to them with other zoos? Animals not in the enclosures? Does it persist if they leave and come back on a different day? A yes to any of these would warrant the classification for the whole zoo, I think.

item.title: Image size
item.content:encoded: The image is currently a little over four megabytes, probably should resize it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An enjoyable read, and a successful first article to boot. Nice job.

Edit: I am wondering, however, if the analysis of 2429 entities proves that the animals really are humans, because you mention their skeleton structure being human-like. Is the analysis being done by those who entered 2429-1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Secain, that it's unclear which it is (altered perception or revealed reality) adds a nice touch of fridge horror.

I like how the zoo employees sort-of know about it, but not entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, this doesn't really go anywhere and the backstory doesn't add much. However, the premise is pretty weird and executed in a good manner, so I do like it on the whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My intention was that it's unclear whether SCP-2429-1 merely alters perception or reveals reality. That's not explicitly stated but I'm considering expanding on this further with experiment logs or an addendum of some kind that might make that angle more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, although I think that either the bathroom door or the perception anomaly should be the actual SCP (and not the zoo per say… unless there is something hidden in between the lines).

item.title: Re: I think it's a nice scip. Pretty funny!
item.content:encoded: Really appreciate the positive response from both of you! If this succeeds it will be my first successful SCP and I'd love to finally contribute.

item.title: Re: I think it's a nice scip. Pretty funny!
item.content:encoded:
I don't think they necessarily need to be separate addenda. They work just fine as part of the description.

With that said, I really like this skip. It has a nice body horror vibe to it, and is an interesting idea itself. I look forward to seeing more from you.

item.title: I think it's a nice scip. Pretty funny!
item.content:encoded:
I'm no elite writer or SCP article artist, but I think it was a nice skip! Well-written, and kept me engaged. The backstory definitely helped!

My suggestion though, would be to add these two sections into separate addendum's, with some possible re-wording so they make sense as separate pieces of information.

"After an intensive Foundation-lead investigation it has been determined that all staff and corporate entities related to SCP-2429 hold no responsibility for the anomalous nature of SCP-2429."

"The barriers to the entrances of SCP-2429-1 seem to have been effective in keeping most out, and an employee only area had eventually been constructed around SCP-2429-1 preventing any guests from potentially finding their way into SCP-2429-1 explaining how SCP-2429 evaded Foundation attention for so long."

+1

item.title: Image Attributions
item.content:encoded:
Image was found on Wikimedia.

Here's the link: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dallas_Zoo_Zebra.jpg#filelinks

Not sure about the original creator of the image. If the link isn't enough some moderator input would be appreciated. I'm a little confused how image attribution works.
